関連科目: Courses on geopolitics, international law, diplomacy/foreign policy of different countries or regions, global studies, global issues, international cooperation
授業の概要・背景:
1. General introductions, definitions
2. IR theories, relevance of history, global political economy and domestic politics for the analysis of international relations events and developments 
3. History of IR - World War I+II, Cold War, post-Cold War unipolar period, current shift in geopolitics incl. case studies
4. Today's world's most pressing global issues and international cooperation incl. case studies 
5. Analysis of past and present international relations events
授業方法: This course will be conducted as a mix of lectures and interaction (seminar/discussion) format. Typically, the topics of each session will be introduced in lecture format (40-50 minutes) and further developed through Q&A/discussions as well as group work. For the duration of the course, students will follow international relations events and developments for discussion/analysis at the beginning of each class.

トピック.授業計画1:
Typically, the sessions' topic will be introduced by the course lecturer in a 40-50 minute presentation followed by Q&A, discussions and group work (mini-assignments in class or based on homework/readings in LUNA).
At the beginning of each face-to-face class (core weeks 1-12), we will look at the past week's main international news/recent developments. This requires the students to follow the news on a daily basis in print/online media, or TV/radio.
The "flex hours" segments of the course will be explained in class; there will be a 30-minute pre-recorded presentation and 60-minute self-study for the two sessions listed as "flex hours".
トピック.授業外学習2: Reading materials, homework assignments and any additional guidance will be shared with the students in class or through the LUNA system. Typically, students will need to spend 1-2 hours every week for reading and completing homework assignments not counting the flex-hour self-study.

教科書/Required texts0: The books listed above will be held on reserve at the KGU library and/or the SIS library that is located on the second floor of the G Building.  -  Additional sources: other relevant articles and reading materials for this course will be provided in class for each session.
教科書/Required texts1: International Relations: A Very Short Introduction
教科書/Required texts3: Karen A. Mingst, Heather Elko McKibben, etc.
W. W. Norton and the Company
教科書/Required texts4: Introduction to International Relations: Theory and Practice
学位授与の方針との関連Relationship to Diploma Polic: All learning goals listed above directly relate to the Diploma Policy.
Feedback and grading for all out-of-class assignments will be entered in LUNA. For any in-class exercises including group work, feedback will be provided on the spot.
成績評価Grading0: 評価基準等　Grading Criteria etc.

Daily short assignments based on the lectures
成績評価Grading3: 授業への参加度（自発性、積極性、主体性、等）／In-class participation,contribution (09)
Presence and participation in class discussions
成績評価Grading4: 備考　Note Rubrics will be attached to assignments in LUNA where applicable. All evaluation criteria will also be explained in class in detail.
備考Note0: In addition to the textbooks listed under "Required Texts" above, some more essential readings are referenced below. Additional relevant reading materials and links to web-articles will be provided in class and through LUNA on an ad-hoc basis.
備考Note1: Political economy analysis is an essential tool for understanding international relations.
備考Note2: Conflict and Cooperation in IR - www.e-ir.info acc231020.pdf
This article talks about the two opposite tendencies in IR: conflict and cooperation.
備考Note3: Realism is one of the two main international relations theories. This article provides a basic explanation of realism in IR.
備考Note4: Realism in IR - ND Int'lSecCenter acc230423.pdf
Realism is one of the two main international relations theories. This article provides a basic explanation of realism in IR.
備考Note5: Liberalism is the second of the two main international relations theories. This article provides a basic explanation of liberalism in IR.
